title: Gold vs Stocks: 50-Year Performance Comparison (1975-2026)
description: What truly happens when you pit gold against the S&P 500 over 50 years? The long-term performance data reveals a shocking reality about inflation and crises you didn't expect.

schemas.headline: Gold vs Stocks: 50-Year Performance Comparison
schemas.description: What truly happens when you pit gold against the S&P 500 over 50 years? The long-term performance data reveals a shocking reality about inflation and crises you didn't expect.

mainEntity.name: How does gold vs stocks performance compare over the last 50 years?

acceptedAnswer.text: Over the past 50 years, the S&P 500 has significantly outperformed gold in both nominal and inflation-adjusted terms, benefiting from dividend reinvestment and corporate earnings growth. However, gold has shown strong performance during specific decades marked by high inflation or market instability.

mainEntity.name: What are the historical gold vs s&p 500 returns?

acceptedAnswer.text: Since 1975, the S&P 500 has delivered a high single-digit to double-digit annualized return, while gold has provided a lower, but positive, long-term return. When adjusted for inflation, the gap in total returns becomes even more pronounced in favor of equities.

mainEntity.name: Should i invest in gold or stocks for long-term growth?

acceptedAnswer.text: For long-term capital appreciation, stocks have historically been the superior asset class. Gold is typically viewed as a store of value or a portfolio diversifier rather than a primary driver of long-term compound growth.

mainEntity.name: When does gold outperform the stock market?

acceptedAnswer.text: Gold tends to outperform the stock market during periods of severe economic crisis, deeply negative real interest rates, and sustained high inflation, such as the late 1970s and the 2000s (the 'Lost Decade' for stocks).

mainEntity.name: Does gold protect against inflation better than the S&P 500?

acceptedAnswer.text: While gold is a traditional inflation hedge and protects purchasing power over centuries, the S&P 500 has historically been a better long-term hedge against inflation, as corporate earnings and dividends tend to rise alongside consumer prices over extended periods.
